title: Why Power Skills Are the Future of Leadership: AI-Powered Microlearning Insights
guestBio: Ioanna Mantzouridou is the founder of Dextego, a groundbreaking platform that uses AI-powered microlearning to help sales teams develop power skills like communication, negotiation, and emotional intelligence. She brings extensive global leadership experience to her entrepreneurial journey, focusing on reshaping the future of learning through innovative technology solutions.
excerpt: What if the key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle, but empathy, confidence, and connection? Discover how AI-powered microlearning is revolutionizing the way we develop essential power skills in just 5-minute learning bursts.
metaDescription: Learn how AI-powered microlearning transforms leadership development. Insights on building empathy, confidence & power skills from Dextego founder.
keyTakeaways: Power skills like empathy, communication, and emotional intelligence are becoming more critical than technical skills in leadership roles.
Microlearning allows busy professionals to develop essential skills in just 5-minute daily sessions, making growth sustainable and manageable.
AI can effectively teach human skills by providing personalized feedback and adaptive learning experiences tailored to individual needs.
Systems thinking and intuition are essential leadership capabilities that women often excel at naturally but need to recognize and develop intentionally.
Scaling human connection in digital environments requires intentional strategies and tools that prioritize authentic relationship building.
The invisible leadership work that women do daily—emotional labor, relationship maintenance, and team cohesion—deserves recognition and strategic development.
Building confidence at work is a learnable skill that can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks.

pullQuotes.text: The key to leadership isn't just strategy and hustle — but empathy, confidence, and connection.

timestamps.label: Ioanna's journey from global leadership to entrepreneurship
Why power skills matter more than ever in business
How AI and microlearning work together
Systems thinking and the invisible work of leadership
Scaling human connection in a digital world

content:
The landscape of leadership is shifting dramatically, and if you're still thinking that technical expertise alone will carry you to the top, you're missing a critical piece of the puzzle. The future belongs to leaders who master what Ioanna Mantzouridou calls "power skills"—those deeply human capabilities like empathy, emotional intelligence, and authentic connection that no algorithm can replicate.

In this transformative conversation, Ioanna shares how her journey from global corporate leadership to founding Dextego opened her eyes to a fundamental gap in how we develop the skills that truly matter. After spending years in executive roles, she recognized that the most successful leaders weren't necessarily the most technically proficient—they were the ones who could connect, communicate, and inspire others with genuine authenticity.

The Revolution of Microlearning

Here's what's revolutionary about Ioanna's approach: she's proving that you don't need hours of training seminars or expensive executive coaching to develop these crucial capabilities. Through AI-powered microlearning, complex skills like negotiation, emotional intelligence, and confident communication can be developed in just five-minute daily sessions. This isn't about dumbing down the learning process—it's about making it sustainable for the reality of your busy life.

The beauty of microlearning lies in its respect for your time and cognitive capacity. Instead of overwhelming you with information you'll forget by next week, it delivers bite-sized insights that your brain can actually process and integrate. When you're juggling leadership responsibilities, family commitments, and personal growth, those five-minute learning moments become powerful catalysts for lasting change.

AI Teaching Human Skills

One of the most fascinating aspects of our conversation centered on how artificial intelligence can effectively teach quintessentially human skills. Ioanna's platform uses AI to provide personalized feedback and create adaptive learning experiences that meet you exactly where you are in your development journey. The AI doesn't replace human connection—it amplifies your capacity for it by helping you recognize patterns, practice responses, and build confidence in real-world scenarios.

This approach acknowledges that developing empathy and emotional intelligence requires practice, feedback, and iteration—just like any other skill. The AI serves as a patient, non-judgmental practice partner that helps you refine your approach until these skills become second nature. It's like having a leadership coach available twenty-four seven, ready to help you navigate complex interpersonal situations with greater wisdom and confidence.

The Invisible Leadership Work

Our conversation took a powerful turn when we addressed the invisible leadership work that women do every day. You know what we're talking about—the emotional labor of maintaining team cohesion, the intuitive reading of group dynamics, the behind-the-scenes relationship building that keeps organizations functioning smoothly. This work is often unrecognized and undervalued, yet it's absolutely essential for organizational success.

Ioanna's insights on systems thinking resonated deeply with this reality. Women leaders often excel at seeing the interconnected patterns and relationships within organizations, understanding how individual actions ripple through entire systems. This capability isn't just valuable—it's becoming indispensable as businesses grapple with increasing complexity and the need for more holistic approaches to problem-solving.

The challenge isn't that you lack these skills—it's that you may not recognize their strategic importance or know how to leverage them intentionally. Systems thinking and intuitive leadership are powerful capabilities that deserve to be developed with the same rigor and attention as any other business skill.

Scaling Human Connection

As our world becomes increasingly digital, the ability to create genuine human connection across virtual platforms becomes a competitive advantage. Ioanna shared valuable insights about how leaders can maintain authenticity and build meaningful relationships even when face-to-face interaction is limited. This isn't about mastering video call etiquette—it's about understanding how to translate your natural leadership presence into digital environments.

The key lies in being intentional about connection. Just as you would deliberately plan a strategy meeting or budget review, scaling human connection requires thoughtful consideration of how you show up, how you create space for others, and how you maintain the emotional threads that bind teams together. Technology should amplify your human capabilities, not replace them.

Building Confidence That Lasts

Perhaps most importantly, our conversation illuminated how confidence—real, sustainable confidence—can be developed through consistent practice and the right learning frameworks. Confidence isn't something you either have or don't have; it's something you build through repeated experiences of competence and growth.

When you combine microlearning with AI-powered feedback, you create a safe space to experiment, fail, learn, and improve. This iterative approach to skill development builds genuine confidence because it's based on actual capability rather than empty affirmations. You become confident because you know you can handle whatever challenges arise, not because someone told you that you should feel confident.

The future of leadership development isn't about choosing between human skills and technology—it's about leveraging technology to amplify your uniquely human capabilities. As Ioanna's work demonstrates, when we combine AI's processing power with human wisdom and intuition, we create learning experiences that are both deeply personal and remarkably effective. The question isn't whether you have time to develop these power skills—it's whether you can afford not to.

quiz.title: What's Your Power Skill Leadership Style?
quiz.description: Discover how you naturally lead with power skills and where your next breakthrough might be hiding. Based on insights from our conversation about the future of leadership.

questions.question: When your team is struggling with a challenging project, what's your first instinct?

options.text: Check in with each person individually to understand how they're really feeling about the situation
Step back and look at the whole process to identify what systemic issues might be causing the struggle
Call a team meeting to openly discuss the challenges and create a clear communication plan moving forward
Try to understand the root emotional and practical needs behind the struggle, then brainstorm creative solutions

questions.question: How do you prefer to develop new leadership skills?

options.text: Through real conversations and observing how different approaches land with people in the moment
By learning frameworks and models that I can apply across different situations and contexts
With consistent, structured practice that builds my confidence through repetition and feedback
By exploring innovative methods that combine traditional wisdom with new approaches and technology

questions.question: What type of leadership challenge energizes you most?

options.text: Building trust and psychological safety in a team that's been through difficult changes
Redesigning processes or structures to help an organization run more effectively
Presenting a vision or leading a difficult conversation that could transform how the team operates
Finding ways to maintain human connection and empathy while scaling or going digital

questions.question: When you think about your biggest leadership strength, it's probably:

options.text: Your ability to sense what's not being said and address the underlying dynamics in any situation
Your talent for seeing how individual problems connect to larger patterns and organizational health
Your willingness to have the hard conversations and communicate clearly even when it's uncomfortable
Your skill at understanding what people need emotionally while also pushing for innovative solutions

questions.question: If you had just 5 minutes a day to grow as a leader, you'd want to focus on:

options.text: Practicing how to better tune into and respond to the unspoken needs of your team
Learning new ways to analyze and improve the systems and processes around you
Building specific communication skills that help you show up more confidently in any situation
Exploring how to blend emotional intelligence with new tools and approaches to leadership

questions.question: What frustrates you most about traditional leadership development?

options.text: It doesn't acknowledge or build on the relationship skills and emotional labor you already do naturally
It focuses on individual tactics instead of helping you think strategically about organizational dynamics
It's often too theoretical and doesn't give you enough practice to actually build confidence in real situations
It hasn't kept up with how technology could enhance rather than replace the human elements of leadership

results.title: The Intuitive Connector
results.description: You have a natural gift for reading between the lines and building authentic relationships that others admire. Your leadership style is rooted in genuine connection, and you often know what your team needs before they say it. While you excel at the invisible work of leadership, you might sometimes doubt whether these 'soft' skills are valued enough. This episode will validate your natural strengths and show you how to leverage AI-powered microlearning to amplify your intuitive leadership gifts.

results.title: The Systems Strategist
results.description: You see the bigger picture and naturally think about how all the pieces fit together in your organization. Your power skill is creating order from chaos and helping others understand complex situations. You're probably already doing systems thinking without even realizing it's a valuable leadership capability. This episode will help you recognize how your strategic mindset is exactly what modern leadership demands and give you tools to develop it further through bite-sized learning.

results.title: The Confident Communicator
results.description: You understand that communication is the foundation of great leadership, and you're willing to put in the work to get better at it. Whether you're naturally confident or still building that muscle, you recognize that how you show up matters as much as what you know. You're ready to invest in yourself consistently. This episode will show you how five-minute daily practices can transform your communication skills and help you build the kind of confidence that inspires others.

results.title: The Empathetic Innovator
results.description: You believe that the best solutions come from truly understanding people's needs and experiences. Your superpower is combining genuine empathy with forward-thinking approaches to leadership challenges. You're curious about how technology can enhance rather than replace human connection. This episode will resonate deeply with you as it explores how AI can actually help develop your emotional intelligence and scale the authentic relationships you value most.

transcript: [Music] Hi everyone and welcome back to the manager the podcast where we reframe invisible labor as the powerful leadership. I'm your host Amy Rickabus, entrepreneur, mother of six and author of the manager. Today's guest is someone I instantly clicked with at a Tomahawk dinner in New York about a year ago. We ended up talking for 3 hours about everything from AI to emotional intelligence and I was just beginning to write my book at the time since she's read the entire thing and I'm honored to have her here today. Iana is the brilliant founder of DexGo, a platform that uses microlearning to build power skills like empathy, confidence, negotiation, and communication in sales teams. She's multilingual, global-minded, and deeply committed to changing the way we think about learning and human development in the workplace. If you're curious about the intersection of AI, education, and what it really takes to be confident in business, this episode is for you. Hi, it's so nice to have you. Thank you so much for coming on the show, Iana. So nice to see you. >> Thank you so much for having me. Lovely to see you always. >> Oh, it's so nice to see you, too. I'm so glad that you had a nice trip, and I'm glad you're back in New York. ready to to get back on track. Yeah. Good. Good. I love it. So, I wanted to start from the beginning with you. If could you take us back to the beginning? How did your background lead you to launching Dexgo? >> Sure. Yeah. So, I think the last what is it now like almost eight years. I've been very much into psychology, talent development, and really my passion has always been around soft skills. And I always knew that that's what drew me to people. That's what um really separates for me the average from the best. Like no matter what your career is, whether you're, you know, a waiter or a CEO or whatever, like at the end of the day, the skills that matter is communication, collaboration, leadership. So I've always been thinking about how can we develop these skills and how can we identify these skills and people that we hire and we work with. For my masters, I studied talent development and strategic management and I worked with a professor who has been researching hyp potential employees and we had very much the same beliefs but then we started researching together and I helped him launch his books and truly understood that stars meaning you know people like LeBron James are very rare um but we all want them right like there's no CEO that will tell you I don't hire the best out there. But the question becomes, it's not about how you attract them. It's how you can develop others to become those stars. Otherwise, you're always going to be fighting for a better salary that someone might give them or more perks or you know, it's it's endless. And on the other hand, when you know how to develop talent, these people become loyal. they grow within your organization and they understand truly the the value ad that you've brought them. So while at my previous company adaptivio uh was working as the VP of people in C of staff and I saw by recruiting and by developing lots of talent that it becomes extremely hard to develop these skills especially in a remote setting because you get to to do this through experience right when you hire someone they get to see how others in the team operate learned by their manager through the examples that they see and Now you just give them a screen and you give them maybe an online course and you're like good luck. And I found that very unfair. Uh I do believe employers have a responsibility to develop talent yet they didn't have the tools to do so. So that's when the idea of DexGo came about an AI coaching platform for softsklls development that can not replace but really fill in the gap because you don't have access to your manager 247. Maybe you're not lucky enough to have a good manager or a safe space to ask questions, but you deserve that place to grow and practice before meeting a client or uh if you're in a customer success for instance role before being yelled at by an angry customer. Long story short, evolved quickly to an AI sales coach where we saw a lot of fraction um because client-f facing roles are the ones that really uh have to develop these skills to build trust with buyers or else they won't be successful in the roles and that has now been almost three years uh that we've had Dexi go out and working on adding more and more um insights in the platform to personal ize the the coaching further at scale. >> Wow. Yeah, that's so cool. when we were talking at dinner um when I met you about a year ago, you were saying how when you have salespeople in a room and maybe you're doing a sales coaching session that when people in a group oftent times the people are afraid to ask questions that would be really important for them to have answered and that you know this is a this having this unique platform where there's no one watching allows your salespeople to ask all the questions that they otherwise wouldn't be confident enough to ask, right? >> And tailored in a way that you understand it. So I always give the example like some people like a coach that's more direct. Some others uh like it more confidence boosting right when you just have online courses or when you just attend a workshop, it's tailored for the average of the group and that by default uh is not touching everyone. So now with AI and understanding truly who you're coaching, you're able to speak to the way that they would listen and you can meet them where they are. Like everyone has different skill sets and everyone will apply them differently based on their day-to-day tasks. So let's say I have a call which is a renewal call but you have a discovery call. We need to work on different skills. So that AI codes now integrated to your workspace to your existing tools um will only keep developing and becoming better as you use it. >> Exactly. Yeah, totally. Which is like this one's a good follow-up question to that. So what sparked your belief that power skills like confidence, listening, and empathy are just as important as technical ones? >> You can quickly tell when you're meeting uh someone and I'll continue with the sales example. the first two minutes if you will ever buy from them or not. And it doesn't matter what they even sell by default. No matter what the technical training they had on the product that they're selling goes out of the room. It doesn't matter. It's irrelevant because if you feel like they're too salesy or too arrogant or they're not listening to you, you're just not going to want to talk to them again. And although this is so true and we all know it, sales organizations have been so focused on training that's about CRM like how do I add my deal in the pipeline? What uh data does it have to have? How do I push it forward? It's really not helpful because you can expect that you hire an 18 19 year old and you give them a phone and you tell them go and talk to a seuite that they've never talked to before without teaching them these soft skills. And that's why I truly think that it's so important to to develop them yet it's so hard because a lot of them are usually developed when you're a kid, right? When you're still in school. >> Oh yeah, absolutely. And I see that with the next book that I'm writing, I definitely feel like there's going to be a lot of that in there as far as, you know, teaching self-confidence, teaching um there are a lot of things that that I learned as a child that I have carried forth into my CEO, my seauite life, you know, but I actually learned them when I was, you know, 2, three, four years old. In fact, my daughter and I were on we're on vacation right now in Hawaii and we were at the pool um at a hotel and my husband and I met a man in the pool and so we shook his hand and introduced ourselves and our daughter, our four-year-old went up to the man and said, "Oh, hi. My name is Indie Rose." And she shook his hand and I thought, >> "I love that." >> Looked him in the eye and shook his hand and I thought, "She's a future CEO. So check her out. Uh, but the whole point is that you can still develop them even as an adult. And I think what you and I both um very much like with this microlearning is that you can start by just focusing on one thing at a time. Like we think that oh my god I have this negative trait I'll never change or this person has that they can never change. Everybody can as long as they do one thing at the time and there is this uh one% improvement every day and collectively then at the end of the year it is indeed someone else. Um but of course you cannot let's say score five out of 10 in negotiation and expect tomorrow you're a 10 out of 10 and that's where I think a lot of people lose faith in the process but it's also because a lot of the trainings have not been based on these principles. You can't expect that you'll give someone a course on negotiation which is passive. They'll have to click next next next to complete it and suddenly they'll they'll become expert negotiators. I mean If that was the case, we would all be hosted negotiators and work in CIA. >> Exactly. So, this one's a good question for those who aren't familiar. What exactly is microlearning and why does it work so well for building soft skills? Because I think a lot of people microlearning kind of blew my mind when you explained it to me. >> Yeah, truly. It is what the word says is this micro steps bite-sized learning every day that ends up collectively them uh teaching you whatever the domain you're you're learning and when it comes to soft skills um let's say we're talking about leadership skills for example what you were saying earlier you can't expect like a 10-year-old to now become the CEO but they're already developing these skills step by step throughout their lives by being in these stress environments where their their skills are being tested then they show up as a leader. So when it comes to uh even math right like we learn a few pieces of geometry a few pieces of uh I don't know something else over the years we're in school similarly with any other topic when you decide to learn it if you break it down um you're going to be able to improve a lot over time and microlarning is something that has been introduced the last years uh in L &D especially in the workplace to be able to feed the busy work day of the employee. Right? We cannot afford to take two hours in a day anymore to learn, but we can afford five minutes prior to a call to do a role play with our AI codes or to read a topic that we're interested in and now apply that in action during the day so you can stick we do it again tomorrow, the next day. And that's how we improve through microlearning. >> I love that. So you blend AI with human behavior in such a unique way. How does DexGo use AI to enhance rather than replace human connection? >> Yes, I love that question. U because it truly is a misconception that AI can do it all for you and it can automate everything. Um I truly believe in the power of a human coach plus AI. I think they provide something very different. I think that a human AI code, a human code, sorry, cannot by default be in every single uh meeting you'll ever be, right? They cannot get data uh from the time they met you till the next time they met you unless you inform them about your progress. So they are lacking that data and analysis but they have much more instinct and they can connect the dots based on their experience and based on patterns that they see from different clients, different organizations, different teams. The AI though is able to very quickly give you answers, improve as you improve, give you uh this byite-size training depending on what's your your goal every time, but it can't really understand super complex situations. You can programmatically say, okay, if user asks for this, give it the samper. And if we're talking about AI agents, do even more complex stuff. But it still is limited to its knowledge base. You can say a human is limited too. But a human has much more creativity and is is resourceful in different ways. So connecting the two I think is the best way to go about it. Um and again as I mentioned earlier our platform for example is just filling a gap that the manager is not accessible that the manager is not approachable and it doesn't mean that the manager is not required. >> Yes. Totally. >> What do you do with the data you have to because you're if you have five minutes in a day it's not going to be the same five minutes that your manager has available. Right. So it's and you how can a manager be available to 200 sales people at once, you know, it's it's asking a lot of a manager to be to have that kind of availability. They're really not. So this gives you a tool >> to be available when your personnel need the ed the micro education. When they have time to do it, >> any time of the day, they can hop on and do their five minutes of learning. And I love I think that it's just powerful. I think microlearning is powerful not just for sales, but I think it's going to be a powerful tool for humanity in the future for lots of things. >> 100%. I mean, look at us. We're always on our phone. So having those five minutes instead of scrolling on social media to learn something, I can be huge. >> I downloaded smartly and I've started doing that when I want when I feel like, oh, I kind of want to look on, you know, social media. I have a few minutes. I'm like, nope. go on smartly. I've been working on my communication skills just because I've been speaking more and all of those, you know, and then doing podcasts and writing and all of those things. And so, it's so neat to have a tool in my pocket that I can learn something new in the 10 or 15 minutes that I have available on that specific day and have it in all languages available. That is another big thing. Like it's not the same if let's say you you're a Spanish speaking person and then your manager doesn't speak Spanish. There is things that can be lost in translation. It's a real thing. >> So very true. Iana and there's so many companies that are global that have employees in multiple countries multiple what work in multiple languages. I mean I can think of my biggest client as global and they're having a huge push in the Spanish language world right now. So, they're going to have so many Spanish language employees that need to have access to tools in their own because when you learn in your own language, it is easier. >> Yes. 100%. >> Yeah. Than trying to do it in your second language. >> Yeah. And you feel like you're more safe to to fail and and learn. >> Yes. I Yeah, that's a great point. So, what has surprised you most about how people learn confidence and communication in short bursts? So what I think is very interesting is when we have like a user who's been doing this for 30 40 years and then they tried and the before and after is very different meaning they will think because we'll ask them like how do you think you'll do on this they rate themselves then AI rates them and you you see immediately that the people who have confidence in their coach ability and ability to improve over time will accept that feedback easily. They will question maybe how did they get that calculation like tell me more, I want to understand it better. But those who lack coachability and have more of a toxic mindset will be very against the result if it's negative for them. like if it's a low score like oh this doesn't work or what is this or I don't agree and it really has surprised me how various people take the feedback um and it actually shows that our hypothesis is valid like your potential of being a high performer is very much connected to your coachability and you're of course you have to have an open mindset and understand that you do have room for growth in order to want to use a tool like dextgo. So the impact uh of confidence in oneself to learning is huge. And on the other hand, if you're not that confident, positive psychology can really help the way you learn because it's another thing for you to practice something and get a response that says you did this wrong, you did this wrong, you did that, okay? Versus you have great potential in this area and this is what you did great, here's how you can improve that. So the way the feedback is delivered is also crucial. It's not just how you give the feedback. It's um sorry, it's not just what the feedback is, but it's how you give it. >> That makes so much sense because some people just don't take criticism well, you know, or or critiquing um or being reviewed, you know, but if you can present it in a way that you're offering them a benefit or giving them a confidence boost. I try to do that with my kids all the time, too. If I'm correcting something, I'll try to do it in a very positive way. Um, I never tell them what not to do. I'll tell them what to do instead. >> There you go. And gamification. Gamification can really help. Like those incentives. I mean, with kids is the same thing. Yeah. Like if you do this right, I'll give you this candy. Like with us is you do something else, you'll get confetti and that dopamine will help them get back to it. Absolutely. I know Indie has a little book. My four-year-old again, she's got a little book and every time she completes an assignment, there's it has stickers in the back and you get to put a little star on it and it's like that's enough for her. >> Just get little things. People are, you know, we like little rewards. We do. >> Mhm. >> Little dopamine rewards that hey, someone sees us. >> Yeah. Absolutely. In a world of distraction, what do you think actually helps someone retain what they learn? >> I think it's uh habit creation. So, back to microlearning, like making sure you do put on the calendar that hey, I'll practice 5 minutes a day, 10 minutes a day. Um, as if you are committing to going to the gym for instance, you have to take the same seriousness in your learning um to really make it sticky. I truly think it has to do also with a learning culture that an organization is creating. So for example, >> you can give them a tool about soft skills but not include soft skills measurements in their performance review because whether we like it or not still people know that they will be measured in certain dimensions at the end of the year and their bondage depends on that. So for them to connect adults and feel incentivized enough to do it, they need to also see uh the benefit in regards to that because you can't think that every employee that you have will be uh self-motivated right you'll have those which is great but you have will you will primarily have people that need a boost. So I think it's creating a learning culture internally, >> making sure you put the time on your calendar for it, and having an accountability partner, whether that's just again an AI thing, maybe sending you a reminder in Slack, or a human just asking you, hey, did you do your 10 minutes a day? Uh, that would really help. >> That is so true. Yeah. You know, it's it's in scuba diving, you always have a scuba buddy. And it's, you know, in life, it's nice to have an accountability buddy for whatever projects you're working on, whether it's, you know, I'm going to do 30 days of yoga in a row or I'm going to write every day for 10 minutes or whatever it is. It really is nice to have a friend come alongside you with that. >> Be nice if the organization buddied up their people maybe to make sure that they're >> doing the work. >> Yeah. >> Yeah. What's the impact I'm having?" And then going back to the day-to-day. Yeah. That's I learned that in child birth. >> I did a a course that was a hypnosis for child birth and we would practice using our imagination to visualize the birth going perfectly well. Um and we would take time every day to do a meditation where we would see the birth going perfectly well because when your brain believes you know imagining is that just as powerful is experiencing to the mind the mind actually doesn't know the difference >> right right and it's visualization that also athletes and founders even uh uses across different things and I've tried that too and I tend to do it uh whenever I remember but my point is that we should actually put it in the schedule too >> as with the microlearning like put time for visualization the neuroscience is there like the facts are there you cannot question the impact it can have so why not benefit from it >> yeah absolutely my life changed in a big way when I started doing this practice on a daily basis when I really made time for myself to see where what I wanted to do next where I really wanted to go in the long term because then when I get stuck in. Oh my gosh, there's a mess. There's a pile of laundry. The kids are crying and I'm stressed out. It's like, >> take a deep breath, see the future, like where we're headed. You know, it's almost like having a heading on your compass >> and making sure you have make sure you have a heading on your compass. Make sure you know where you want to go.
